summary: I live in Aurangābād, in the same house where my family has gathered for generations. Each morning, I rise early to prepare the puja altar, arranging the flowers and incense just as my mother taught me. Our home is filled with familiar Marathi decor—brass lamps, handwoven mats, and framed photographs of ancestors—reminders of the traditions I hold dear. Though retired since the early 1990s, my days remain structured. I oversee the household routines, ensuring that the younger family members respect the customs passed down to us. I choose trips that immerse me in India’s rich cultural heritage, visiting temples and historic sites where I can feel the weight of history and tradition. I avoid flashy tourist spots, preferring places where rituals are still performed with reverence. In the kitchen, I prepare Maharashtrian dishes using recipes handed down through generations. The aroma of goda masala and fresh kokum brings the family together, reinforcing the ties that bind us. My life is woven tightly with the customs and values of my people, and I guard them carefully, day by day.

I insist on strict check-ins with my team, not because I distrust them, but because I believe that control and clear lines of authority keep operations running smoothly. When I visit vendors, I’m meticulous—every detail matters, from delivery times to product quality. It’s this attention to hierarchy and order that has shaped my career over more than thirty years. I’m cautious about new technology. While others rush to adopt the latest digital tools, I prefer methods that let me maintain a firm grip on processes. Manual checks and rigid schedules might seem old-fashioned, but they give me the confidence that nothing slips through the cracks. This approach extends beyond work; I’m conservative with my personal finances, favoring secure investments over risky ventures. It’s a mindset that has served me well, even if it means I’m slower to embrace change. Outside the office, I keep to myself more than most. My leisure time is often spent reading industry reports or catching up on local news rather than socializing. I enjoy strategic board games—they challenge my planning skills and let me unwind while still engaging my mind. Meals are simple and familiar, usually traditional Goan dishes that remind me of home and routine. I’m aware that my insistence on control and hierarchy can make collaboration difficult. I prefer clear roles and responsibilities rather than open-ended teamwork. Aging hasn’t softened this; if anything, it has reinforced my belief that vigilance and discipline are necessary to stay relevant. Each evening, after ensuring everything is in order, I prepare for the next day, knowing that maintaining this structure is what keeps both my work and life steady.
